current_fact.fact_text: On April 20, 1985, activists from the Animal Liberation Front raided a University of California, Riverside laboratory, freeing 467 animals used in experiments and causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage. Their night‑time operation ignited fierce debate about the ethics of animal testing and the limits of protest.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

current_fact.match_reason: A radical act driven by empathy for animals and moral conviction sits alongside an episode spotlighting powerful voices who question, challenge, and stand firm in their beliefs.

queue.fact_text: On April 20, 2013, a magnitude 6.6 earthquake struck Lushan County in Sichuan, China, shaking many of the same mountains hit by a deadly quake five years earlier. Entire villages were jolted awake as landslides, collapsed buildings, and aftershocks reminded residents how restless the ground beneath them really is.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: After a violent shaking of the Earth, a gently paced, downtempo episode offers a sonic counterweight—a reminder of the need to slow down, breathe, and re‑ground after upheaval.

queue.fact_text: On April 20, 2015, a bomb struck a convoy bringing food to a United Nations compound in Garowe, the capital of Somalia’s Puntland region. The attack killed ten people, including UN staff, and highlighted the risks faced by those trying to deliver help in fragile regions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A somber attack on humanitarian workers is paired with a moody, cinematic trip‑hop journey whose reflective, shadowed textures mirror the gravity of conflict zones.

queue.fact_text: On April 20, 2007, a gunman entered building 44 at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, taking hostages before killing one engineer and himself. In a place devoted to exploration and optimism, the shooting exposed the human stress and fragility that can exist even inside a symbol of scientific progress.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A tragedy inside a high‑tech space center connects with an episode that examines the psychological strain of modern technology and the unseen pressures within advanced environments.

queue.fact_text: On April 20, 2023, SpaceX’s Starship—the most powerful rocket ever built—lifted off from Texas, only to explode four minutes into flight when its stages failed to separate. Far from being dismissed as a failure, the fiery spectacle was treated as a full‑scale test, inching humanity closer to fully reusable, interplanetary rockets.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A giant rocket chasing a cosmic future fits naturally with a 'Cosmic Monday' of space‑tinged electronic disco, celebrating experiments that don’t go perfectly but push boundaries forward.

queue.fact_text: On April 20, 1809, Austrian and French forces clashed near Abensberg; nearly two centuries later on the same date in 1961, another kind of clash unfolded as U.S. and Soviet blocs eyed each other in the Cold War’s shadow. The day carries a pattern of confrontations where strategy, ideology, and human cost collide on shifting fronts.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: A day marked by geopolitical confrontation, especially the Cold War, connects with an episode about Residente, whose work is steeped in political critique, resistance, and the human side of ideological conflict.

queue.fact_text: On April 20, 2010, as the Deepwater Horizon oil spread across the Gulf, scientists raced to track its movement using satellites, sensors, and ocean models. The disaster turned the ocean into a vast, tragic laboratory for understanding how currents carry both life and pollution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

queue.match_reason: The Gulf spill’s slow, spreading waves and the need to listen carefully to the ocean tie in with a dub‑soaked episode built on echo, space, and a deep, tidal sense of rhythm.

queue.fact_text: On April 20, 2015 in Garowe, even as a UN convoy was attacked, local people continued ordinary acts of care—sharing food, rebuilding, and adapting. The day became a stark reminder that in many places, daily life and danger exist side by side, with resilience woven quietly through both.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: A day of violence in a fragile city connects with an episode on healing, recovery, and the small, steady acts that help people continue after trauma.

queue.fact_text: Across many Aprils 20th, from Pasteur’s flasks to Starship’s test flight, the date keeps catching moments when experiments don’t go as planned but still move knowledge forward. It’s a day that quietly celebrates trial, error, and the strange beauty of imperfect attempts that change how we see the world.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

queue.match_reason: A meta‑reflection on failed yet fruitful experiments is perfectly matched with an episode about wabi‑sabi—the beauty of imperfection, rough edges, and things that don’t go according to plan.

published.fact_text: In 1972, Apollo 16’s Lunar Module Orion touched down on the Moon’s rugged highlands, a region scientists once thought too risky to land on. Astronauts John Young and Charles Duke spent nearly three days exploring the Descartes Highlands, bringing back rocks that proved the area wasn’t volcanic as previously believed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A lunar landing that redefined our view of the Moon pairs with an episode about reframing empty space as creative potential and shifting perspectives.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 1902, Pierre and Marie Curie refined radium chloride, isolating one of the most radioactive substances then known. Their painstaking work in a cramped shed lit the way for modern nuclear physics, cancer radiotherapy, and a deeper understanding of the invisible energies woven through matter.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The Curies’ discovery of powerful, unseen energy matches an episode themed around electrifying shocks and high-voltage rock.

published.fact_text: In 1862, Louis Pasteur and Claude Bernard completed a series of simple but elegant flask experiments that toppled the age‑old idea of spontaneous generation. By showing that microbes came from other microbes, not from “nothing,” they helped give birth to modern microbiology and sterile technique.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A careful rethinking of how life begins aligns with an episode about learning, unlearning, and the joy of overturning old assumptions.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 1865, Italian astronomer Angelo Secchi demonstrated a simple white disk lowered into the sea that could measure how clear the water was. This Secchi disk, tested from Pope Pius IX’s own yacht, became one of oceanography’s earliest tools for peering into the transparency—and health—of our waters.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A tool for literally measuring how deep we can see into water complements an episode themed around immersion, fluidity, and moving below the surface.

published.fact_text: In 1861, balloonist Thaddeus S. C. Lowe drifted 900 miles from Cincinnati to South Carolina in a daring attempt to prove the value of aerial observation. His record-breaking flight helped convince the U.S. military that hot-air balloons could be eyes in the sky long before satellites and drones.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A long, experimental journey through the air resonates with an episode recorded on a boat, centered on travel, movement, and shared adventure across distances.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 1828, French explorer René Caillié slipped into the fabled city of Timbuktu disguised as a Muslim trader, becoming only the second non-Muslim to enter it—and the first to make it home alive. His quietly detailed accounts punctured centuries of myth that painted Timbuktu as a city of pure gold.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: A perilous journey to Timbuktu on the edge of the Sahara fits a desert‑rooted episode about Tuareg culture, sand, and survival in the same region.

published.fact_text: In 1770, Georgian king Erekle II, abandoned by his Russian ally, faced the Ottoman army at Aspindza and won. Outnumbered, he chose a risky surprise attack that became a symbol of Georgia’s stubborn independence wedged between empires.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: A defiant stand against empires mirrors an episode celebrating rock musicians who refuse permission and fight for space with loud, uncompromising energy.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 1809, Napoleon used rapid maneuvers at the Battle of Abensberg to split and defeat two Austrian army corps in Bavaria. It was the second day of a four‑day campaign where speed and surprise mattered more than sheer numbers—and it set the stage for a broader French victory.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: A fast, decisive campaign that changes the course of a war pairs with the explosive first episode that launched Sedna FM’s own bold offensive into space‑radio.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 1861, Robert E. Lee resigned his commission in the United States Army, turning down command of Union forces to instead lead Virginia’s troops. His choice, rooted in loyalty to his home state over the federal government, became one of the American Civil War’s most consequential personal decisions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: Lee’s controversial choice between conflicting identities and loyalties reflects an episode about labels, identity, and the difficult act of stepping out of expected boxes.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 1961, the Bay of Pigs Invasion collapsed as U.S.-backed Cuban exiles were defeated on the beaches of Cuba. The failed covert operation strengthened Fidel Castro’s position, pushed Cuba closer to the Soviet Union, and became a textbook example of miscalculation in the Cold War.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A failed invasion born of ideology and power struggle aligns with an episode centered on political resistance and the sonic rage of confronting empires.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 1945, twenty Jewish children who had been subjected to brutal medical experiments at Neuengamme were murdered in the basement of Hamburg’s Bullenhuser Damm school. The killing was a final attempt by the SS to erase evidence of their crimes as the war drew to a close.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A profoundly dark moment near the end of World War II is paired with a reflective, rain‑themed episode that leans into grief, transition, and the hope that storms, however heavy, do eventually end.

published.fact_text: Also on April 20, 1945, U.S. troops captured the German city of Leipzig, only to hand it over to Soviet forces weeks later under Allied agreements. For residents, the liberation was followed by another abrupt shift in occupation, foreshadowing the coming divide of postwar Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

published.match_reason: A city caught between powers and historical shifts echoes an episode about a different industrial 'steel city,' with music capturing the grit, identity, and multiple edges of urban life.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 1949, the British sloop HMS Amethyst was shelled and trapped on the Yangtze River while trying to reach the embassy in Nanjing, in what became known as the Amethyst incident. For months, the ship lay stranded under Chinese artillery, turning a routine passage into a tense diplomatic standoff on the water.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A warship trapped on a river during a fraught voyage connects with a reflective, landscape‑driven episode whose tracklist even includes a sailors’ song about life at sea.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 2010, the Deepwater Horizon drilling rig exploded in the Gulf of Mexico, killing 11 workers and triggering the largest marine oil spill in history. For nearly six months, crude oil gushed into the ocean, coating coasts and wildlife and forcing the world to confront the true cost of deep‑sea drilling.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A catastrophic oil spill threatening marine life pairs with an ocean‑centered episode that celebrates whales and underwater worlds, underscoring what’s at stake in those waters.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 2004, a section of Singapore’s Nicoll Highway suddenly collapsed into a massive excavation for a new subway line, killing four workers. The disaster exposed how complex underground engineering can be—and how much depends on invisible layers of soil, support, and calculation beneath a city’s smooth surface.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: A hidden structural failure beneath a modern city pairs with an episode about paying attention to what lies underneath habits—shifting from automatic systems to conscious, intentional choices.

published.fact_text: On April 20, 1985, activists from the Animal Liberation Front raided a University of California, Riverside laboratory, freeing 467 animals used in experiments and causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage. Their night‑time operation ignited fierce debate about the ethics of animal testing and the limits of protest.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: A radical act driven by empathy for animals and moral conviction sits alongside an episode spotlighting powerful voices who question, challenge, and stand firm in their beliefs.
